title: Initial PTSD - Research-Derived Non-Public DBQ Substitute

public_availability.status: Not available for public use according to VA public DBQ inventory
public_availability.caveat: This is not an official VA DBQ and should not be presented as one. It is a structured research aid built from public VA/eCFR criteria and adjacent public examination concepts.

source.agency: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs / eCFR
source.research_method: Derived from VA public non-available DBQ list, public DBQs with similar exam structure, and 38 CFR Parts 3 and 4 criteria.

purpose: Prepare truthful facts for an initial PTSD exam where VA restricts the official DBQ.

diagnosis_current_condition: What condition, diagnosis, residual, or claimed symptom pattern is currently present?
When did it begin, who diagnosed or treated it, and what medications, devices, procedures, therapy, testing, or hospitalizations are documented?
What current objective findings, records, labs, imaging, audiology, neuropsychological testing, or clinician observations support it?
nexus_service_connection: What in-service event, injury, illness, exposure, captivity, deployment, hazardous duty, blast, acoustic trauma, or aggravation is claimed?
What dates, locations, units, MOS/duties, incidents, STR entries, personnel records, buddy statements, or exposure records support the event?
If secondary or aggravated, what service-connected condition caused or worsened this condition, and what medical evidence supports that relationship?
functional_impact_severity: How does the condition affect work attendance, task performance, concentration, communication, mobility, self-care, sleep, relationships, and daily activities?
What are baseline limitations versus flare-up or episode limitations?
Use truthful examples with frequency, duration, severity, triggers, treatment response, and missed/modified work.

rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.frequency_duration: How often symptoms/episodes occur and how long they last.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.severity_scale: Best, typical, and worst severity; use 0-10 only when clinically sensible, especially pain.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.work_daily_life_impact: Concrete examples such as missed work, accommodations, difficulty hearing speech, memory problems, lifting/sitting/standing limits, ADLs, or relationship effects.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.flare_ups: Triggers, frequency, duration, what worsens, what functional loss occurs, and what helps.

diagnosis_current_condition: Current PTSD diagnosis must conform to DSM-5 and be made by a qualified mental health examiner.
Identify whether there are additional mental diagnoses and whether symptoms can be differentiated.
Document treatment, medications, hospitalizations, therapy, safety issues, substance use history, and relevant medical/psychiatric history.
medical_history_onset_course_treatment: Pre-military, military, and post-military mental health/social/work history.
nexus_service_connection_prompts: Identify claimed PTSD stressor(s), date/location/unit, whether combat/MST/fear/hostile activity/trauma applies, and corroborating evidence or reports.
symptoms_signs_findings: Intrusive memories, nightmares, flashbacks, distress or physiological reactivity to reminders.
Avoidance of memories, thoughts, feelings, people, places, conversations, or activities.
Negative mood/cognition symptoms: guilt, shame, detachment, persistent negative beliefs, diminished interest, inability to feel positive emotions.
Arousal/reactivity symptoms: irritability, anger, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration problems, sleep disturbance, reckless/self-destructive behavior.

Panic, depression, anxiety, suspiciousness, memory impairment, impaired judgment/thinking, suicidal ideation, obsessional rituals, impaired impulse control, neglect of hygiene, disorientation, hallucinations/delusions when present.
Frequency, severity, duration, remissions, treatment response, and functional impact of psychiatric symptoms.
diagnostic_testing_objective_findings: Qualified mental health examination using DSM-5 criteria.
Review of stressor evidence, STRs/personnel records, deployment/MST/fear-of-hostile-activity facts, treatment records, prior diagnoses, and lay statements.
Risk/safety assessment if suicidal/homicidal ideation, psychosis, severe impairment, or crisis signs are present.
frequency_duration_prompts: Negative mood/cognition symptoms: guilt, shame, detachment, persistent negative beliefs, diminished interest, inability to feel positive emotions.
Frequency, severity, duration, remissions, treatment response, and functional impact of psychiatric symptoms.
severity_prompts: Negative mood/cognition symptoms: guilt, shame, detachment, persistent negative beliefs, diminished interest, inability to feel positive emotions.

Panic, depression, anxiety, suspiciousness, memory impairment, impaired judgment/thinking, suicidal ideation, obsessional rituals, impaired impulse control, neglect of hygiene, disorientation, hallucinations/delusions when present.
Frequency, severity, duration, remissions, treatment response, and functional impact of psychiatric symptoms.

Occupational and social impairment level: work efficiency, reliability/productivity, judgment, thinking, mood, family relations, school, social isolation, hygiene, safety, ability to adapt to stress, and ability to maintain relationships.
functional_impact_prompts: Occupational and social impairment level: work efficiency, reliability/productivity, judgment, thinking, mood, family relations, school, social isolation, hygiene, safety, ability to adapt to stress, and ability to maintain relationships.
Missed work, conflicts, reduced productivity, panic at work, inability to tolerate crowds/noise, sleep-related fatigue, concentration/memory errors, relationship strain.
Effects on parenting, marriage, friendships, public places, driving, weapons safety, and self-care.

sections.title: SECTION 1 - SOURCE AND AVAILABILITY
items: VA lists this DBQ as not available for public use.
Use this JSON as a preparation aid, not as a completed DBQ.

sections.title: SECTION 2 - DIAGNOSIS / CURRENT CONDITION
items: Current PTSD diagnosis must conform to DSM-5 and be made by a qualified mental health examiner.
Identify whether there are additional mental diagnoses and whether symptoms can be differentiated.
Document treatment, medications, hospitalizations, therapy, safety issues, substance use history, and relevant medical/psychiatric history.

sections.title: SECTION 3 - MEDICAL HISTORY AND NEXUS FACTS
items: Pre-military, military, and post-military mental health/social/work history.
Trauma/stressor timeline and symptom onset/progression.
Identify claimed PTSD stressor(s), date/location/unit, whether combat/MST/fear/hostile activity/trauma applies, and corroborating evidence or reports.

sections.title: SECTION 4 - SYMPTOMS, SIGNS, AND FINDINGS
items: Intrusive memories, nightmares, flashbacks, distress or physiological reactivity to reminders.
Avoidance of memories, thoughts, feelings, people, places, conversations, or activities.
Negative mood/cognition symptoms: guilt, shame, detachment, persistent negative beliefs, diminished interest, inability to feel positive emotions.
Arousal/reactivity symptoms: irritability, anger, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration problems, sleep disturbance, reckless/self-destructive behavior.

Panic, depression, anxiety, suspiciousness, memory impairment, impaired judgment/thinking, suicidal ideation, obsessional rituals, impaired impulse control, neglect of hygiene, disorientation, hallucinations/delusions when present.
Frequency, severity, duration, remissions, treatment response, and functional impact of psychiatric symptoms.

sections.title: SECTION 5 - TESTING / OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items: Qualified mental health examination using DSM-5 criteria.
Review of stressor evidence, STRs/personnel records, deployment/MST/fear-of-hostile-activity facts, treatment records, prior diagnoses, and lay statements.
Risk/safety assessment if suicidal/homicidal ideation, psychosis, severe impairment, or crisis signs are present.

sections.title: SECTION 6 - FUNCTIONAL IMPACT
items: Occupational and social impairment level: work efficiency, reliability/productivity, judgment, thinking, mood, family relations, school, social isolation, hygiene, safety, ability to adapt to stress, and ability to maintain relationships.
Missed work, conflicts, reduced productivity, panic at work, inability to tolerate crowds/noise, sleep-related fatigue, concentration/memory errors, relationship strain.
Effects on parenting, marriage, friendships, public places, driving, weapons safety, and self-care.

sections.title: SECTION 7 - SPECIAL NOTES
items: If the veteran is in crisis, contact emergency services or Veterans Crisis Line 988 then press 1.

recommended_user_inputs: Current diagnosis or claimed symptom/residual pattern
Exact onset date/timeframe and in-service event/exposure/injury/captivity/deployment/acoustic/blast facts
Treatment history, medications, devices, therapy, surgeries, hospitalizations, specialist care, tests, and records
Baseline symptoms with frequency, duration, severity, and daily functional impact
Flare-ups/episodes/attacks with triggers, duration, severity, and what activities become impossible or limited
Work impact, accommodations, missed work, safety issues, communication limits, memory/concentration issues, ADL limits, and relationship effects
cheat_sheet_use.truthfulness_guardrail: Use only accurate personal facts. Do not invent symptoms, diagnoses, stressors, captivity details, exposures, test results, or medical nexus opinions.

script_templates.truthfulness_guardrail: Fill in only what is true. Skip wording that does not apply. Do not exaggerate, invent symptoms, stressors, exposures, test results, or medical nexus opinions.

default_options.duration: hours at a time, with sleep disruption carrying into the next day
default_options.triggers: trauma reminders, crowded stores, restaurants, traffic, fireworks/loud noises, conflict, anniversaries, or poor sleep
default_options.limitation: sleep, concentration, mood, relationships, crowds, motivation, and work reliability
default_options.treatment: mental health treatment, therapy tools, medication if prescribed, grounding, isolation, and support from trusted people
default_options.flareCare: step away, ground myself, use paced breathing, isolate briefly, take medication if prescribed, and contact support
default_options.serviceEvent: [specific service event, injury, exposure, aggravation, or secondary condition]
default_options.workImpact: At work I avoid crowded rooms and unexpected contact, lose focus after intrusive thoughts or poor sleep, get irritable when startled or pressured, and may need to step outside or leave early to prevent escalation.
default_options.dailyImpact: At home I may sleep separately or wake my partner, avoid stores/restaurants/events, sit with my back to a wall, isolate after conflict, and fall behind on chores or hygiene during bad weeks.
default_options.mainSymptoms: intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance, hypervigilance, irritability, panic, poor sleep, concentration problems, and relationship strain if true
default_options.evidence: service records, treatment records, VA/private records, medications, imaging/labs/testing, profiles, buddy statements, or lay statements if true

sections.title: Medical History / Onset & Nexus
sections.intent: Answer when/where symptoms started, what service event/exposure/aggravation may connect them, and what treatment has been tried.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: current mental health diagnosis, onset, treatment history, medication/therapy, and symptom course; in-service event, injury, illness, hazardous exposure, aggravation, or secondary service connection timeline; therapy, medication, crisis/safety care if true, coping tools, support systems, and mental health follow-up
sections.what_to_say_template: My claimed condition is {{currentDiagnosis}}. It first started or became noticeable {{onset}} after {{serviceEvent}}. Since then, the condition has continued with {{mainSymptoms}}. I have tried {{treatment}}, but symptoms still happen {{frequency}}. Evidence that may support the timeline includes {{evidence}}.

sections.title: Symptom Evaluation
sections.intent: Answer DBQ-style symptom questions with exact frequency, duration, severity, triggers, and condition-specific symptoms.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: occupational and social impairment, sleep, mood, panic, judgment, thinking, relationships, motivation, memory, and ability to handle stress
sections.what_to_say_template: My symptoms are {{mainSymptoms}}. They happen {{frequency}}, usually last {{duration}}, and are triggered or worsened by {{triggers}}. On better days they are about {{bestPain}}; on worst days they reach {{worstPain}}. For example, symptoms happen {{frequency}}, with worse episodes after {{triggers}}, and sleep problems can affect the next day.

default_options.duration: hours at a time, with sleep disruption carrying into the next day
default_options.triggers: trauma reminders, crowded stores, restaurants, traffic, fireworks/loud noises, conflict, anniversaries, or poor sleep
default_options.limitation: sleep, concentration, mood, relationships, crowds, motivation, and work reliability

subsections.title: Frequency & Duration
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Document frequency, duration, pattern, triggers, and whether symptoms are constant, intermittent, recurrent, or episodic.
subsections.what_to_say_template: My {{currentDiagnosis}} symptoms are {{frequency}}. They usually last {{duration}}. The symptoms I need documented are {{mainSymptoms}}. They get worse with {{triggers}}.
subsections.example_template: For example, symptoms happen {{frequency}}, with worse episodes after {{triggers}}, and sleep problems can affect the next day.

subsections.title: Severity Scale
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe severity using measurable words, worst-day impairment, and any DBQ-specific severe-episode language that truthfully applies.
subsections.what_to_say_template: On better days, severity is about {{bestPain}}. On worst days, it reaches {{worstPain}}. Overall, I would describe it as {{severity}} because it interferes with {{limitation}}.
subsections.example_template: On a bad day, symptoms reach {{worstPain}} and I have to stop or reduce {{limitation}} instead of pushing through.

bullet_templates: Sleep: nightmares, waking in sweat/panic, broken sleep, or only [__] hours if true
Anxiety/panic: crowds, stores, traffic, work meetings, loud noises, or reminders
Mood/behavior: irritability, anger outbursts, isolation, depression, low motivation, or guilt
Memory/focus: forgetting tasks, rereading instructions, losing track in conversations

sections.title: Functional Impact / Occupational Assessment
sections.intent: Answer how the disability limits work, daily activities, relationships/social function, reliability, and worst-day functioning.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: occupational and social impairment, sleep, mood, panic, judgment, thinking, relationships, motivation, memory, and ability to handle stress

subsections.title: Daily Activities
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Explain work limitations, daily activity limits, missed time, accommodations, safety concerns, and reduced reliability/productivity.
subsections.what_to_say_template: This affects work because {{workImpact}} It affects daily life because {{dailyImpact}} I can usually continue for about {{limitBeforeStopping}} before I need to stop, rest, modify the task, or get help.
subsections.example_template: A real-life example is: poor sleep, anxiety, irritability, low motivation, or concentration problems make me less reliable and I may need isolation, breaks, or schedule changes.

subsections.title: Flare-Ups / Worst Days
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe flare-up frequency, triggers, duration, worst-day functional loss, and what helps relieve symptoms.
subsections.what_to_say_template: Flare-ups or worst episodes happen {{flareUps}}. They are triggered by {{triggers}} and can last {{duration}}. During them, I cannot keep up with {{limitation}}, and I use {{flareCare}}.
subsections.example_template: During a flare-up, symptoms spike to {{worstPain}}, I avoid people or situations, may leave the room/store/work area, and I {{flareCare}} to calm down and prevent things from getting worse.

bullet_templates: Work: leaving meetings, avoiding coworkers/customers, needing breaks, mistakes, missed days
Home: sleeping separately, avoiding family events, neglecting chores/hygiene, isolating in a room
Public places: grocery stores, restaurants, crowds, fireworks, traffic, or sitting with back to a wall
Worst days: panic, anger, shutdown, no sleep, not leaving home, or needing support to calm down
